title: Anomia: cancerul real al României
summary: The article argues that Romania's deepest problem is not its budget deficit but a pervasive moral bankruptcy—anomie—characterized by the erosion of social trust, moral standards, and legitimate leadership. Drawing on Leo Srole's concept and Hannah Arendt's 'banality of evil,' the author contends that Romanians have normalized corruption, abuse, and institutional failure, retreating into tribal subgroups amplified by social media algorithms. While acknowledging that current leaders like PM Bolojan are not corrupt, the author warns they are treating symptoms (fiscal deficit) rather than the root cause (societal anomie).
talking_points: Romania's real crisis is moral collapse (anomie), not just the budget deficit
Romanians have internalized the 'banality of evil'—cycling through brief social media outrage before accepting systemic corruption and abuse
Anomie triggers two responses: authoritarian tendencies (explaining the sovereignist movement) and social contraction into tribal subgroups fueled by social media bubbles
Poll data shows catastrophic trust deficits: 82% distrust politicians, 70.5% distrust the judiciary, and 76% believe the country is heading in the wrong direction
Current leaders focus on fiscal symptoms rather than the underlying social disease
author: Andrei Colțea
